policy_statement: By 2030, create incentives for renewable diesel and emerging zero-emission technologies to cut emissions from construction equipment at least 50% from 2020 levels (TLU.7-6).
mechanism_description: Creates incentives to increase use of renewable diesel and zero-emission technologies in construction equipment, reducing emissions from that equipment.
primary_causal_pathway: Construction equipment activity → fuel/powertrain shift (renewable diesel/zero-emission) → ↓ fossil fuel combustion & lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Construction contractors/operators adopt renewable diesel or zero-emission construction machinery (e.g., battery-electric, hydrogen fuel cell) instead of conventional diesel equipment, driven by incentives. (2) Process: Reduced direct CO₂ (and often CH₄/N₂O on a lifecycle basis) from fossil diesel combustion and/or reduced upstream emissions from fuel production; electrification can shift energy demand to the grid where emissions are typically lower and can decline over time. (3) Outcome: Lower net greenhouse gas emissions from the construction equipment fleet (and typically co-pollutant reductions as a co-benefit).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from construction equipment operation due to switching fuels and powertrains away from fossil diesel.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: incentives drive adoption of lower-carbon fuels and zero-emission technologies for construction equipment, leading to lower GHG emissions from equipment energy use.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: the primary intent and largest effect is direct/lifecycle GHG reduction, not reduced energy use per se (Resource Efficiency), hazard reduction (Adaptation), or ecosystem-mediated benefits (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: renewable diesel can vary in lifecycle carbon intensity; if incentives unintentionally increase equipment utilization, some reductions could be offset, but the dominant expected pathway remains emissions reduction per unit activity and/or via fleet turnover.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy under this mechanism (incentivizing renewable diesel and/or zero-emission construction equipment) centrally works by lowering GHG emissions from equipment operation, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., electrification vs low-carbon liquid fuels, air-quality co-benefits) is deferred to policy-level evidence and implementation specifics.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Lifecycle emissions depend on renewable diesel feedstock and accounting method; electrification benefits depend on grid carbon intensity and charging logistics; policies may also deliver significant air-quality benefits, but those are secondary to the mitigation pathway.
key_indicators: “create incentives”; “renewable diesel”; “zero-emission technologies”; “reduce emissions from construction equipment”; “at least 50% from 2020 levels”; “By 2030”
co_benefits: Reduced air pollutants (e.g., NOx, PM) and improved local air quality from lower diesel combustion.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes a target (“reduce…at least 50%”) but does not specify a mandatory requirement; classification is based on the stated instrument (“create incentives”) rather than a performance standard or mandate.
